Current Opinion in

Structural Biology

Mechanistic insights into INO80-type chromatin remodelers Gregory D. Bowman Chromatin remodelers are adenosine triphosphate (ATP)driven enzymes that physically reorganize nucleosomes, the basic packaging unit of all eukaryotic chromosomes. INO80, SWR1/SRCAP, and TIP60 are large multisubunit remodelers that share similar components yet have distinct biochemical and biological functions. This review summarizes key architectural features of these complexes and how they engage DNA, nucleosomes, and hexasomes to carry out their tasks. Addresses TC Jenkins Department of Biophysics, Johns Hopkins University, Baltimore MD, 21218, USA Corresponding author: Bowman, Gregory D. (gdbowman@jhu.edu)

Current Opinion in Structural Biology 2025, 92:103030 This review comes from a themed issue on 3D Genome Chromatin Organization and Regulation (2025) Edited by Yawen Bai and Yuan He For complete overview of the section, please refer the article collection 3D Genome Chromatin Organinzation and Regulation (2025) Available online 27 March 2025

however, SWR1/SRCAP alters the composition of the histone core, exchanging canonical H2A/H2B histone dimers with H2A.Z/H2B variants [11,12]. A third INO80 family complex, called TIP60 in vertebrates and DOM-A in flies, appears to be an evolutionary fusion of the yeast SWR1 and NuA4 complexes [10]. Although TIP60 has been shown to acetylate histones via the NuA4 HAT module, it typically fails to provide robust H2A.Z exchange [11e15]. It is therefore unclear if the Snf2 ATPase of TIP60 requires stimulation by auxiliary factors for H2A.Z exchange, or if it has been repurposed for an as-yet undiscovered activity. INO80-type remodelers are large complexes with more than a dozen subunits. These remodelers have been extensively studied with regard to their composition, structures, biochemistry, and biology, which have been excellently described in several recent reviews [10,16e19]. This review focuses on more recent structural and single molecule studies that have greatly advanced our mechanistic understanding of these remarkable machines.

https://doi.org/10.1016/j.sbi.2025.103030 0959-440X/© 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

Introduction Chromatin remodelers help determine the occupancy, positioning, and identity of histone cores throughout eukaryotic genomes. They are classified into different families based on a helicase-like translocase that binds and distorts duplex DNA, called a Snf2 ATPase motor [1,2]. Despite sharing subunits and common architectural features, the INO80 family is defined by three remodeling complexeseINO80, SWR1/SRCAP, and TIP60ewith distinct functions (Figure 1). INO80 can reposition nucleosomes [3,4] as well as the related hexasomes [5], which lack one H2A/H2B histone dimer. In many genes, INO80 properly positions the first nucleosome in the gene body (called þ1) that flanks the promoter, using DNA sequence to fine-tune nucleosome placement [6e8]. Also targeting the þ1 nucleosome [9], a second INO80 family remodeler complex is called SWR1 in yeast, SRCAP (SNF2-related CBP (CREB binding protein) activating protein) in mammals, and DOM-B in flies [10]. Instead of repositioning,

The Rvb1/2 ring provides a framework for recognizing histones wrapped by DNA A defining feature of INO80-type remodelers is the inclusion of a hexameric AAAþ ring made of alternating Rvb1 (also called RuvBL1, TIP49a, or Pontin) and Rvb2 (RuvBL2, TIP49b, Reptin) subunits [12,20]. The Rvb1/ 2 hexamer provides a platform to spatially organize other subunits. Although the Rvb1/2 hexamer is symmetric, the symmetry is broken upon association with the Snf2 ATPase subunit. In addition to coordinating the Snf2 ATPase, the Rvb1/2 hexamer is also adorned with subunits unique to each remodeler type, several of which have long extended segments that snake through the assembly. The unique subunits appear to have a significant hand in conferring key characteristics for each remodeler type. INO80 and SWR1/SRCAP remodelers use a common 2 pronged surface for engaging with nucleosomes and hexasomes (Figure 2). One prong is the Snf2 ATPase itself, which engages almost exclusively with the DNA duplex. The other is either Arp5 (INO80) or Arp6 (SWR1/SRCAP) paired with another subunit (Ies6 for INO80, Swc6/ZNHIT1 for SWR1/SRCAP). Both the

[Page 2]

Functions of INO80-type remodelers. (a) Diagrams highlighting the composition and organization of nucleosomes and hexasomes. (b) Schematic representation of a hypothetical gene segment packaged into nucleosomes. (c) Remodeler actions: INO80 positions +1 nucleosomes and can shift hexasomes; SWR1/SRCAP exchanges H2A.Z/H2B dimers into +1 nucleosomes; TIP60 acetylates histones H4 and H2A.

Snf2 ATPase and Arp5/6 pairs are oriented and held apart by the Rvb1/2 hexamer, such that they match the topology of DNA wrapped around histones. Unlike INO80 and SWR1/SRCAP, nucleosome/hexasome binding has not yet been observed for TIP60. Although sharing many subunits with SWR1/SRCAP, TIP60 lacks the Arp6/Swc6 pair and thus is expected to have a distinct means of nucleosome recognition. Remarkably, despite a similar overall organization, INO80 and SWR1/SRCAP engage with nucleosomes in distinct rotational orientations (Figure 2a). In SWR1/ SRCAP, the Snf2 motor engages at superhelix location 2 (SHL-2), or 3 o’clock relative to the nucleosome dyad [21e23], whereas for INO80, the motor instead binds DNA at SHL-6 or SHL-7 (9e10 o’clock) [24,25]. This rotational difference is also mirrored for the Arp5/Arp6 pairs, which roughly bind on opposing edges of the nucleosome at SHL-6/-7 (Arp6 and Swc6/ZNHIT1 for SWR1/SRCAP) or SHL-3 (Arp5 and Ies6 for INO80). For all other chromatin remodelers studied to date, the Snf2 ATPase preferentially binds to the nucleosome at SHL2, so the distinct orientation of INO80 on the nucleosome makes it unique. This difference appears to Current Opinion in Structural Biology 2025, 92:103030

be due in large part to a helical insertion in Arp5, called the grappler, which contacts both the acidic patch on the H2A/H2B dimer and nucleosomal DNA between the dyad and SHL-1 (Figure 3a). Another major component of these remodeling complexes is the so-called Arp module (distinct from Arp5/ Arp6), which consists of Arps, actin, and other subunits that assemble along the long helical helicase-SANTassociated (HSA) domain, N-terminal to the Snf2 ATPase. For INO80 and SRCAP, a common feature of the Arp module is DNA binding. For INO80, this module, called the Arp8 module, binds to DNA flanking the nucleosome [7,26], whereas for mammalian SRCAP, the Arp module has been visualized bound to DNA that wraps around the proximal H2A/H2B dimer [22,23] (Figure 2a,b). For yeast SWR1, although this Arp module appears to possess a DNA-binding surface similar to SRCAP, at low resolution it appeared to arch away from nucleosomal DNA and suggested a distinct mode of interaction that may be dominated by histone tail binding [27]. For INO80, mammalian SRCAP, and yeast SWR1 remodelers, the Arp module is flexibly attached to the ATPase motor, often making it difficult to

[Page 3]

Structural commonalities and differences among INO80 family members. Shown on the left are footprints of how various remodeler subunits contact histones and DNA. Note how INO80 has a similar presentation of the Snf2 ATPase and Arp5, yet binds nucleosomes and hexasomes in distinct orientations, as indicated by the locations of the dyad (black triangles). For TIP60, a complex of the Snf2 ATPase with nucleosomes or hexasomes has not yet been observed. On the right are molecular representations of INO80-type assemblies, aligned based on the Rvb1/2 ring (PDB codes: INO80 nucleosome, 8AV6; INO80 hexasome, 8OO7; SWR1 nucleosome, 9B1E; SRCAP nucleosome, 8X19; SWR1 hexasome, 8QYV; TIP60 Rvb1/2 hexamer and Arp module, 9CAC).

visualize in conjunction with the Rvb1/2 hexamer. For TIP60, however, the Arp module stably binds to the Rvb1/2 hexamer [13,14,23,28]. This binding to the

Rvb1/2 hexamer is incompatible with the mode of DNAbinding observed for the Arp modules of SRCAP and INO80 [13,14,23,28].

[Page 4]

Elements that sense the histone core. (a) Select subunits in an INO80-nucleosome complex, highlighting interactions of the Arp5 grappler with the H2A/H2B dimer and acidic patch (PDB code 8AV6). (b) Select subunits in an INO80-hexasome complex, showing how the Arp5 grappler is positioned in a similar fashion on DNA and against histone H4, both exposed by the missing H2A/H2B dimer (PDB code 8OO7). Note the similar overall organization of the INO80 ATPase and Arp5/Ies6 as on the nucleosome (a), but in a distinct rotational setting. (c) Interactions of SWR1 with the hexasome, highlighting a long helix from Swc5 that extends into the space left by the missing H2A/H2B dimer (PDB code 8QYV).

INO80 stays at the edge In recent work, both INO80 and SWR1 have been visualized bound to hexasomes [29e31]. Surprisingly, INO80 shifts its orientation on hexasomes relative to nucleosomes, whereas SWR1 does not (Figure 2a). This reorientation of INO80 appears to result from topological recognition of the histone-DNA core by both the ATPase motor and the Arp5 grappler. On hexasomes, INO80 reorients such that the ATPase motor is bound at SHL-3 and Arp5/Ies6 is bound over the dyad (Figure 3b). The Arp5 grappler contacts DNA at SHLþ2/þ3, which is exposed upon loss of the H2A/ H2B dimer, and a helical segment of the grappler sits in the pocket previously occupied by the H2A/H2B dimer, appearing to mimic the H2BeH4 contact seen in the nucleosome. Intriguingly, INO80 requires an intact acidic patch on the proximal H2A/H2B dimererecognized by part of the grapplereto robustly slide nucleosomes [24,32], yet this requirement is waived when sliding hexasomes [30]. When INO80 binds the nucleosome, the Snf2 motor sits at the nucleosome edge, the so-called entry/exit site, where flanking DNA transitions to being wrapped around the histone core [20,24]. In the hexasome, the Snf2 motor again sits at the edge, but with the absence of one H2A/H2B dimer, the DNA wrapping begins at a more internal location on the H3/H4 tetramer [30,31]. Although it was proposed that INO80 may transform nucleosomes into hexasomes during sliding, disruption of the H2A/H2B dimer is not a prerequisite for nucleosome sliding, as shown using nucleosomes with H2A/ H2B cross-linked to each other [5,29,31]. Based on Current Opinion in Structural Biology 2025, 92:103030

DNA gap experiments, INO80 requires intact DNA between SHL-2 and SHL-6 to robustly slide nucleosomes [33], yet gaps at SHL-6 do not affect sliding of hexasomes [30,31]. This differential sensitivity to DNA gaps suggests that INO80 treats hexasomes and nucleosomes as distinct entities and simply orients on each such that the Snf2 motor acts where the DNA starts wrapping the histone core. INO80 requires flanking DNA on the entry side (the DNA being pulled onto the histone core) for nucleosome sliding [3,4,34,35]. For INO80, the sensor for flanking DNA is the Arp8 module, which sits just outside the entry/exit site of the nucleosome [7,26,33]. The Arp8 module not only senses the length of available DNA, it also modulates remodeling activity based on DNA sequence [7,8]. This regulatory control via the Arp8 module apparently stems from the direct connection to Snf2h ATPase, where the long HSA helix that serves as the platform for Arp8 components directly plugs into the Snf2 ATPase motor [7,17]. Interestingly, on hexasomes, the Arp8 module is also observed on flanking DNA, where it is in a similar activating orientation relative to the Snf2 motor [30,31]. When given the same amount of flanking DNA, INO80 does not appear to prefer hexasomes over nucleosomes [30]. However, since the loss of an H2A/ H2B dimer liberates w40 bp, INO80 would preferentially slide a hexasome formed from a nucleosome within 40 bp of neighboring nucleosomes, since the additional DNA would more easily allow the hexasomes to pass the w50 bp threshold of flanking DNA required for maximal sliding [4,30,31].

[Page 5]

SWR1 interactions with DNA and histones. (a) Interactions of yeast SWR1 with naked DNA [27]. (b) Predicted interactions of the Swc3 subunit with flanking DNA [27]. (c) Swc2 binds to flanking DNA on both sides of the nucleosome [38]. (d) CryoEM class averages showing the canonical and intermediate positions of nucleosomes bound to SWR1. (e) Interactions of SWR1 and the Chz1 chaperone with the deposited H2A.Z dimer [29]. Figure panels adapted from Refs. [29,38], and [27] with permission.

SWR1 uses auxiliary subunits to target DNA flanking the +1 nucleosome In yeast, both SWR1 and H2A.Z histones are localized to the þ1 nucleosome for many genes [9]. A key feature flanking the þ1 nucleosome is the long flanking DNA of the nucleosome-depleted region (NDR) (Figure 1b), which SWR1 depends on for binding and H2A.Z deposition [36]. A cryoEM structure shows that, unexpectedly, yeast SWR1 engages free DNA with both the Snf2 motor and Arp6, the same sites that grip the nucleosome [27] (Figure 4a). Presumably this DNA interaction helps SWR1 scan promoter DNA in search of the þ1 nucleosome [37]. Through the characteristically long flanking DNA of the promoter, yeast SWR1 targets the þ1 nucleosome with its Swc2 and Swc3 subunits. Swc3 binds to Arp6/Swc6, seated near entry/exit DNA [27]. With modeling, Swc3 is expected to contain a long helix that could extend along naked DNA outside the nucleosome (Figure 4b). Consistent with promoter targeting, deletion of Swc3 in yeast leads to a loss of SWR1 targeting to þ1 nucleosomes, with the spreading of both SWR1 and H2A.Z into

coding regions [27]. Swc2, which is necessary for incorporation of Swc3 and stabilization of Arp6/Swc6, is essential for H2A.Z exchange [27,36], and Swc2 deletion also results in genomic redistribution of SWR1 outside the þ1, as well as SWR1-independent spreading of H2A.Z [9]. As shown in recent structures, a central segment of Swc2 packs against the Rvb1/2 hexamer, allowing it to interact with flanking DNA on either side of the nucleosome [38] (Figure 4c).

Exchanging histones on the flipping nucleosome The observations that both Swc2 and Swc3 bind to flanking DNA provide important mechanistic insight into a perplexing problem. Although SWR1 recognizes flanking DNA on one side of the þ1 nucleosome, it deposits H2A.Z on both sides [9,11]. So how is the double deposition accomplished? Through single molecule Förster Resonance Energy Transfer (smFRET) experiments, nucleosomes were found to flip relative to the SWR1 remodeler without remodeler release [38]. This nucleosome flipping was also captured by cryoEM (Figure 4d), showing how SWR1

[Page 6]

accesses both copies of the H2A/H2B dimer sequentially in two reactions [38]. The kinetics of nucleosome flipping was also found to be sensitive to the substrate: with one copy of H2A replaced by H2A.Z, the remodeler preferentially releases the H2A.Z face, allowing it to spend more time on the face with canonical H2A. SWR1 is known to not exchange H2A.Z/H2B dimers already on the nucleosome [11], and this bias away from the H2A.Z-containing side may provide proofreading to help ensure specificity [38]. A necessary intermediate of the histone replacement process is a hexasome, lacking one of the two H2A/H2B dimers. As shown by single molecule experiments and cryoEM structures, SWR1 binds tightly and specifically to the hexasome intermediate, which significantly biases the orientation of the histone core so that the side that will receive the new H2A.Z/H2B dimer is poised for histone addition [29,38]. In the structure, yeast SWR1 inserts a helical segment of the Swc5 subunit into the pocket made by removal of the canonical H2A/H2B dimer, reminiscent of the INO80 grappler placement against H4 (Figure 3b,c) [29]. A subsequent intermediate of histone exchange was also captured with an H2A.Z/H2B dimer cross-linked to the histone chaperone Chz1, with the dimer in the expected position on the nucleosome yet with the DNA still unwrapped as in the hexasome [29]. In that intermediate, the presence of Chz1 appears to block DNA rewrapping, and the H2A.Z/H2B dimer is additionally contacted by N-terminal segments of the Snf2 ATPase and Swc5 C-terminus (Figure 4e).

TIP60 – wearing a new HAT As a fusion of SWR1/SRCAP and NuA4 complexes, TIP60 has even greater complexity and potential functionality than other INO80-type remodelers. The NuA4 component adds a histone acetyltransferase (HAT) module, the TINTIN (trimer independent of NuA4 involved in transcription interactions with nucleosomes) module, and the large (>3700 residue) TRRAP (transformation/transcription domain-associated protein) subunit needed for genomic targeting of the complex. Interestingly, the NuA4 complex alone also incorporates a similar Arp module as found in the INO80-type remodelers [39]. In NuA4, the Arp module is stably bound to the TRRAP subunit [40e42] and nucleosome recognition is achieved through the HAT module [43]. In TIP60 structures, the Arp module is instead bound to the Rvb1/2 hexamer, and the TRRAP component is flexibly attached through the C-terminus of the Snf2 ATPase subunit, EP400 [13,14,23,28]. Despite the presumed dual functionality of TIP60, it has not yet been possible to visualize nucleosome binding through its SWR1/SRCAP-like core [23,28]. Subunits of the TIP60 Arp module have been reported to cross-link to histones, but the cross-linking sites are incompatible Current Opinion in Structural Biology 2025, 92:103030

with SRCAP-nucleosome complexes, suggesting that nucleosome binding would require a large conformational change [14]. So far, nucleosome binding to TIP60 has been only observed through the HAT module, similar to that seen in the NuA4-nucleosome complex [28,43]. Thus, despite the emerging understanding of SWR1/SRCAP complexes, the means by which TIP60 may engage with nucleosomes to promote H2A.Z exchange remains enigmatic.

Conclusions and future questions The great strides in uncovering key mechanistic features of INO80-type remodelers have reframed and focused many of our concepts of how these machines work. With our new understanding and appreciation of these systems, we can now address burgeoning new questions: For INO80, how and where does the remodeler target hexasomes, how is this process regulated, and what outcome is achieved through hexasome sliding? For SWR1/SRCAP, how does the Snf2 ATPase, which distorts DNA, contribute to the H2A.Z exchange reaction, and what is the role of the Arp module in histone exchange? Finally, for TIP60, does the Snf2 ATPase promote H2A.Z exchange or have another function, what conformational changes occur for the Arp module, and how does subunit and module reorganization affect function? Answering these questions and more is likely to reveal new surprises and give us a deeper appreciation of this multifunctional remodeler family.

Mol Cell 2022, 82: 2098–2112. e4. Krietenstein N, Wal M, Watanabe S, Park B, Peterson CL, Pugh BF, Korber P: Genomic nucleosome organization reconstituted with pure proteins. Cell 2016, 167:709–721.

Kunert F, Metzner FJ, Jung J, Höpfler M, Woike S, Schall K, Kostrewa D, Moldt M, Chen J, Bantele S, Pfander B, Eustermann S, Hopfner K: Structural mechanism of extranucleosomal DNA readout by the INO80 complex. Sci Adv 2022, 8, eadd3189. This study describes how the Arp8 module and HSA domain of INO80 bind duplex DNA. DNA geometry is sensed by a combination of the Arp8 module, the Snf2 ATPase motor, and the grappler element of Arp5, with stiff DNA specifically adjacent to the Snf2 ATPase slowing nucleosome repositioning. The authors describe how the Arp8 module, through the HSA connection, could directly control action of the Snf2 ATPase, analogous to the SWI/SNF family of remodelers.

Oberbeckmann E, Krietenstein N, Niebauer V, Wang Y, Schall K, Moldt M, Straub T, Rohs R, Hopfner K, Korber P, Eustermann S: Genome information processing by the INO80 chromatin remodeler positions nucleosomes. Nat Commun 2021, 12: 3231 [z]. Using in vitro biochemistry coupled with deep sequencing, the authors demonstrate that positioning by the INO80 remodeler is sensitive to DNA shape, specifically propeller twist. They further show that nearby transcription factors such as Reb1, as well as DNA ends (as may be encountered during DNA damage) are strong barriers for nucleosome positioning by INO80.

Poterszman A, Schultz P, Papai G, Ben-Shem A: Structure of the human TIP60-C histone exchange and acetyltransferase complex. Nature 2024, 635:764–769. This study describes the TIP60 structure, which closely matches those of [Yang 2024; Chen 2024; Park2024]. Like others, the authors conclude that the TRRAP module is flexibly attached to the module containing the Rvb1/2 hexamer, Snf2 ATPase, and Arps. They show that H2A.Z exchange activity is less dependent on flanking DNA length than SWR1, and that acetylation appears to promote exchange. 14. This work, along with [23], shows with cryoEM how the Arp module wraps around the nucleosome in conjunction with other subunits of the SRCAP complex. Park G, Patel AB, Wu C, Louder RK: Structures of H2A.Z associated human chromatin remodelers SRCAP and TIP60 reveal divergent mechanisms of chromatin engagement. bioRxiv 2024:1–28, https://doi.org/10.1101/2024.07.30.605802. This work reports cryoEM structures for SRCAP/nucleosome and TIP60 complexes. Along with [Chen et al., 2024], the authors show that when the Snf2 ATPase and Arp6/ZNHIT1 are engaged with the nucleosome, the Arp module of SRCAP organizes along the outer DNA gyre, bordering the target H2A/H2B dimer to be exchanged. Ranjan A, Höllmüller E, Stengel F, Pugh BF, Wu C: Molecular basis of global promoter sensing and nucleosome capture by the SWR1 chromatin remodeler. Cell 2024, 187: 6849 – 6864. This work reports cryoEM structures of SWR1 bound to DNA and nucleosomes, showing different encounter complexes. The authors predict that Swc3 extends into and helps read out long DNA segments flanking the nucleosome, which is supported by a swc3 knockout where both SWR1 and H2A.Z redistribute from +1 nucleosomes to gene bodies. 28. Chen K, Wang L, Yu Z, Yu J, Ren Y, Wang Q, Xu Y: Structure of

the human TIP60 complex. Nat Commun 2024, 15:7092 [z]. The authors report structures of the TIP60 complex, similarly described by [Park 2024; Li 2024; Yang 2024], which show that the Arp module packs against the Rvb1/2 hexamer, unlike NuA4 where the Arp module packs against Tra1 (human TRRAP). They reveal how the core Snf2 ATPase, called EP400, is the central scaffold holding components together: it is a platform for the Arp module, threads through the Rvb1/2 ring, and its Cterminus binds to the TRRAP subunit, which is flexibly attached. 29. Mol Cell 2024, 84:3871–3884. e9.

[Page 8]

Here the authors report several cryoEM structures representing key intermediates during H2A.Z exchange. They show how SWR1 engages with a hexasome and captures the incoming H2A.Z/H2B dimer while still bound to the Chz1 chaperone. 30. Zhang M, Jungblut A, Kunert F, Hauptmann L, Hoffmann T, Kolesnikova O, Metzner F, Moldt M, Weis F, DiMaio F, Hopfner K, Eustermann S: Hexasome-INO80 complex reveals structural basis of noncanonical nucleosome remodeling. Science 2023, 381:313–319. This paper, along with [Wu et al., 2024], showed the INO80 remodeler bound to a hexasome, revealing that the orientation of the hexasome differs from that of a nucleosome substrate. In addition, they visualize the Arp5 grappler, which packs against histone H4 and DNA that becomes exposed in hexasomes compared to nucleosomes. 31. Wu H, Muñoz EN, Hsieh LJ, Chio US, Gourdet MA, Narlikar GJ, Cheng Y: Reorientation of INO80 on hexasomes reveals basis for mechanistic versatility. Science 2023, 381:319–324. This work captured the structure of the INO80 remodeler on a hexasome, which also shown by [Zhang et al., 2024], is in a different orientation than on a nucleosome. The Snf2 ATPase appeared dynamic in cryoEM structures and was observed at a range of locations from SHL-3 to SHL-2 on the hexasome. 32. Girvan P, Jalal ASB, McCormack EA, Skehan MT, Knight CL, Wigley DB, Rueda DS: Nucleosome flipping drives kinetic proofreading and processivity by SWR1. Nature 2024 Dec, 636:251–257. The authors used single molecule FRET to reveal that nucleosomes flip over while bound to the SWR1 remodeler. The kinetics of nucleosome reorientation correlates with the nucleosome face nearest the Swr1 ATPase, with faster flipping promoted by H2A.Z/H2B, and stable binding with a missing dimer of a hexasome. The authors also visualized intermediates in the flipping reaction with cryoEM. 39. Nat Struct Mol Biol 2023, 30:1337–1345. 43. Qu K, Chen K, Wang H, Li X, Chen Z: Structure of the NuA4

acetyltransferase complex bound to the nucleosome. Nature 2022, 610:569–574. This study describes cryoEM structures of the yeast NuA4 complex bound to a nucleosome. The nucleosome is bound by subunits in the HAT module, indicating how histone H4 is targeted for acetylation. The DNA flanking the nucleosome is close to the TRRAP subunit, suggesting how upstream activator sequences may help recruit and orient the complex.
